name: select_llm_candidates
body:
"""
    Collect needs_llm findings into a batched validate_findings() input.

    Args:
      duplicates / intent_mismatches: raw detector dicts (as produced by
        api.models.duplicates / api.models.intent).
      body_by_id: function id ("file::name") → source body, for snippets.
      max_candidates: cap on the DUPLICATE candidates in the Claude batch.
      max_intent: reserved cap on the INTENT candidates (kept separate so a
        flood of duplicates can't starve the lie detector).

    Returns (llm_input, refs):
      llm_input: list of {id, question, snippet_a, snippet_b, confidence}
                 — the exact shape AnthropicClient.validate_findings wants.
      refs:      parallel list of (kind, source_index) where kind is
                 "duplicate" | "intent" and source_index points back into
                 the matching input list, so verdicts fold back in place.

    Duplicates and intents are capped INDEPENDENTLY (so intent always gets a
    verdict), then combined and ordered by confidence descending for a
    deterministic batch.
    """
    # (confidence, kind, source_index, question, snippet_a, snippet_b)
    dup_pool: list[tuple[float, str, int, str, str, str]] = []
    intent_pool: list[tuple[float, str, int, str, str, str]] = []

    for idx, d in enumerate(duplicates):
        if not d.get("needs_llm"):
            continue
        a = d.get("function_a", "")
        b = d.get("function_b", "")
        sim_pct = round(float(d.get("similarity", 0)) * 100)
        dup_pool.append((
            float(d.get("confidence", 0)),
            "duplicate",
            idx,
            f"Are {a} and {b} doing the same thing? Similarity: {sim_pct}%",
            body_by_id.get(a, ""),
            body_by_id.get(b, ""),
        ))

    for idx, m in enumerate(intent_mismatches):
        if not m.get("needs_llm"):
            continue
        name = m.get("name", "")
        fid = m.get("function_id", "")
        sim_pct = round(float(m.get("similarity", 0)) * 100)
        intent_pool.append((
            float(m.get("confidence", 0)),
            "intent",
            idx,
            f"A function is named {name}() but its name-vs-body similarity is "
            f"only {sim_pct}%. Does {name}() actually do what its name claims? "
            f"CONFIRMED = the name is misleading; REJECTED = the name fits. "
            f"In your explanation, say what the function ACTUALLY does.",
            body_by_id.get(fid, ""),
            "",
        ))

    # Cap each kind independently (highest confidence first), then combine and
    # re-sort so the batch + refs are deterministic. Stable sort keeps duplicates
    # ahead of intents at equal confidence.
    dup_pool.sort(key=lambda t: t[0], reverse=True)
    intent_pool.sort(key=lambda t: t[0], reverse=True)
    selected = dup_pool[:max_candidates] + intent_pool[:max_intent]
    selected.sort(key=lambda t: t[0], reverse=True)

    llm_input: list[dict[str, Any]] = []
    refs: list[tuple[str, int]] = []
    for i, (conf, kind, idx, question, snip_a, snip_b) in enumerate(selected):
        llm_input.append({
            "id": i + 1,
            "question": question,
            "snippet_a": snip_a,
            "snippet_b": snip_b,
            "confidence": conf,
        })
        refs.append((kind, idx))

    return llm_input, refs

name: apply_verdicts
body:
"""
    Fold Claude verdicts back into the finding lists.

    `verdicts` is parallel to `refs` (both parallel to the select_llm_candidates
    input). For each:
      confirmed → stamp llm_verdict + boost confidence to CONFIRMED_CONFIDENCE
                  (so the CLI promotes it past the 0.85 high-confidence bar)
      rejected  → mark the source finding for removal (false positive)
      uncertain → stamp llm_verdict, leave confidence untouched

    Confirmed/uncertain findings are mutated in place (confidence bump +
    llm_verdict tag). Returns (kept_duplicates, kept_intent_mismatches) with
    rejected findings filtered out — order otherwise preserved.
    """
    drop_dup: set[int] = set()
    drop_intent: set[int] = set()

    for v, (kind, idx) in zip(verdicts, refs):
        verdict = str(v.get("verdict", "uncertain")).lower()
        target = duplicates[idx] if kind == "duplicate" else intent_mismatches[idx]
        target["llm_verdict"] = verdict
        # Carry Claude's one-line justification — for intent this is the "what it
        # ACTUALLY does" payload that turns a flag into a readable lie report.
        target["llm_explanation"] = str(v.get("explanation", ""))[:240]

        if verdict == "confirmed":
            target["confidence"] = max(
                float(target.get("confidence", 0)), CONFIRMED_CONFIDENCE
            )
        elif verdict == "rejected":
            (drop_dup if kind == "duplicate" else drop_intent).add(idx)

    kept_dups = [d for i, d in enumerate(duplicates) if i not in drop_dup]
    kept_intents = [m for i, m in enumerate(intent_mismatches) if i not in drop_intent]
    return kept_dups, kept_intents

"""
    Verify the Stripe webhook signature and return the parsed event.
    Raises ValueError on bad payload, stripe.error.SignatureVerificationError on bad sig.

    Signature verification only needs STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET — it deliberately
    does NOT call _ensure_stripe(), so the webhook can validate even when no
    API key is configured (the handlers themselves will surface that
    separately when they reach a Stripe API call).
    """
    if not STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET:
        raise RuntimeError("STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET is not configured")
    import stripe
    return stripe.Webhook.construct_event(payload, sig_header, STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET)
